documents.title: Stony Brook University public AI hub or guidance
Stony Brook University AI policy/guidance source

ai_strategy.summary: Stony Brook University's public AI posture is visible through governance guidance, teaching resources, research activity, and enterprise/tool support. The evidence points to a pragmatic R1 approach: encourage AI literacy and research while constraining sensitive data and academic-integrity risks.

key_provisions: Use AI in ways consistent with academic integrity and existing university policy.
Protect sensitive, confidential, student, research, or regulated data when using AI tools.
Faculty or academic units retain responsibility for clear course-level expectations where applicable.
Human review and accountability remain necessary for AI-assisted work.
ai_policy.summary: Stony Brook University has public AI guidance or policy-adjacent materials. The cited guidance addresses responsible use, data protection, ethics, and course or work-context expectations.

bodies.name: AI Coordination and Communication Work Group
bodies.scope: Coordinates or evidences institutional AI governance, guidance, research, teaching, or tool enablement.

governance_structure.summary: Public evidence identifies AI Coordination and Communication Work Group as the main AI governance or coordination signal for Stony Brook University.

initiatives.title: AI Coordination and Communication Work Group AI governance and guidance
initiatives.description: Stony Brook University publishes AI guidance or governance resources that frame responsible use, academic integrity, privacy, and institutional oversight. The cited materials make AI a university-level issue rather than only a classroom technology topic.
initiatives.significance: This is a material public signal of how an R1 university is translating AI from discussion into institutional practice.

investment.description: No public dollar amount was identified for this specific initiative.

collaborations.description: No external collaboration was identified in the cited source.

initiatives.policy_implications: The public guidance creates an institutional baseline for course, research, and administrative AI use.

initiatives.title: NVwulf AI GPU cluster
initiatives.description: Stony Brook launched NVwulf, a GPU cluster designed for AI/ML and data-intensive research with 24 H200 GPUs in phase one and later B40 expansion.
initiatives.significance: This closes compute capacity with a named AI-specific GPU cluster and public GPU count.

investment.description: NVwulf page describes a $5 million New York State grant supporting the multi-year initiative.

collaborations.description: State funding supports the AI and Technology program associated with NVwulf.

initiatives.title: NSF national supercomputer for AI access
initiatives.description: NSF awarded Stony Brook and University at Buffalo $13.77 million to procure and operate high-performance, energy-efficient computing for AI research and broader cyberinfrastructure participation.
initiatives.significance: This closes cost/funding and access gaps with a large public award tied to expanding AI compute participation.

investment.description: NSF grant to deploy national AI and research computing infrastructure.

initiatives.title: Leveraging AI in program assessment workshop
initiatives.description: Stony Brook Office of Educational Effectiveness published a workshop deck on leveraging AI in program assessment.
initiatives.significance: This adds direct assessment-redesign evidence rather than only generic teaching guidance.
initiatives.policy_implications: Uses AI to support program assessment workflows.

computing_infrastructure.summary: NVwulf is Stony Brook’s dedicated GPU cluster for AI/ML and data-intensive research. Phase one added 24 NVIDIA H200 NVL GPUs; January 2026 notices added three B40 nodes with four RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell GPUs each, bringing the public NVwulf GPU count to at least 36.

investments_and_gifts.purpose: Deploy a national supercomputer to democratize access to AI and research computing with University at Buffalo.

investments_and_gifts.purpose: Support AI and technology program development, including NVwulf GPU cluster investment.

student_facing_ai.name: Generative AI campus hub and DoIT resources

equity_and_access_initiatives.title: Stony Brook University responsible AI and access signal
equity_and_access_initiatives.description: Cited public materials connect AI adoption to privacy, bias, academic integrity, access, community benefit, or responsible human use.

regulatory_context.summary: New York public AI activity is relevant context; this profile treats campus policy and institutional evidence as the primary governance layer.

narrative_summary: Stony Brook University presents an AI posture typical of an active R1 institution: public guidance for generative AI, visible teaching resources, research activity, and a growing set of supported tools or infrastructure. The most mature signals are governance and responsible-use guidance, teaching and syllabus support, and research capacity through AI Innovation Institute. AI and computer science policy/program resources gives the institution a workforce-development or curriculum signal, while Generative AI campus hub and DoIT resources shows that AI is also being handled as an enterprise technology and data-governance issue. Public sources also show attention to responsible use, access, bias, privacy, or community impact, though some operational details remain incomplete. In particular, current public pages do not consistently disclose named executive ownership, all procurement decisions, full user counts, or complete dollar amounts. This profile therefore treats the institution as operational and growing rather than fully transformative: AI is embedded across policy, pedagogy, research, and tools, but not every part of the campus AI operating model is publicly documented.

gaps_acknowledged: Leadership names, enrollment, and exact enterprise AI user counts were not filled unless directly necessary; public pages were stronger for initiatives than current-role rosters.
Some initiatives cite policy or hub pages because a single dated strategy document was not publicly identified.
No comprehensive AI budget was identified beyond explicitly cited grants or public funding signals.
Stony Brook now has public AI compute and funding evidence, but Worker 3 did not find public campus-wide LLM platform user counts or contract values.
Public sources emphasize democratized compute access, but subgroup-level student outcomes from AI adoption were not disclosed.

strategic_archetype.rationale: Primary archetype selected from visible public signals: research compute powerhouse.
